points: When electric current flows through a conductor, it produces a magnetic field around it—a phenomenon discovered by Hans Christian Oersted in 1820. The magnetic field can be detected using a magnetic compass needle, which deflects when brought near the current-carrying wire; it returns to its original position when the current stops.

An electromagnet is a current-carrying coil that behaves like a magnet. Its strength depends on the amount of current flowing through it, the number of turns in the coil, and whether it has an iron core. An electromagnet has two poles (North and South) like a bar magnet, and its polarity can be reversed by changing the direction of electric current.

Electromagnets have practical applications in lifting electromagnets (used in cranes for factories and scrap yards), electric bells, motors, fans, and loudspeakers. Earth's magnetic field is generated by the movement of liquid iron in its core, creating electric currents that help organisms navigate and protect life from harmful space particles.

When electric current flows through any conductor, it faces resistance to its flow. This resistance converts some electrical energy into heat energy. The amount of heat generated depends on the material of the wire, its thickness, length, the magnitude of the electric current, and the duration of current flow.

Nichrome wire, which offers higher resistance than copper wire of the same size, is commonly used in electrical heating devices. Household appliances that use the heating effect include electric room heaters, stoves, electric irons, immersion rods, kettles, hair dryers, and incandescent lamp filaments.

While the heating effect is useful in household appliances and industrial furnaces (like those for steel manufacturing), it can be dangerous if not controlled. Overheating can damage plugs and sockets or cause fires, so appropriate wires and safety devices rated for specified current must be used in household circuits.

A Voltaic cell generates electricity through chemical reactions between two different metal electrodes and an electrolyte (weak acid or salt solution). This principle was discovered by Alessandro Volta and applied by Luigi Galvani. Voltaic cells can be made from materials like lemons, copper wire, and iron nails, with the liquid or juice acting as the electrolyte.

Dry cells are practical, portable alternatives to Voltaic cells. They contain a zinc container (negative terminal), a carbon rod (positive terminal), and a paste-like electrolyte. Dry cells are single-use; once the chemicals are depleted, the cell becomes 'dead' and must be replaced.

Rechargeable batteries can be charged and discharged multiple times, reducing waste and saving money. Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries are the most common rechargeable batteries today, used in phones, laptops, and electric vehicles. After repeated charging cycles, rechargeable batteries eventually wear out. Used batteries must be recycled in special e-waste facilities because they contain hazardous materials and valuable metals that can harm the environment if improperly disposed.
